chapters.title: — Cold Open: The Webpage That Didn't Change
— The Evidence Trail: What Memory Actually Costs Now (with Maren)
— The Evidence Trail: What Survived (and What It Costs)
— The Complication: Intel's Gambit (and Why Caspar Is Skeptical)
— The Complication: Valve's Retreat That Might Be a Strategy
— Synthesis: What the $399 Handheld Actually Was
— Synthesis: The Two Markets That Don't Have Names Yet
— Reflective Ending: The Platform That Outlasted the Price

follow_up_links.title: OneXPlayer 3 launching globally in June 2026 with Intel Arc G3 Extreme and 144 Hz OLED display

boundaries: Should not predict exact DRAM prices or specific recovery timelines with false precision
Should not characterize any specific manufacturer's business decisions as intentional harm
Should not claim insider knowledge of fab construction plans beyond what is publicly reported
Should not frame AI infrastructure investment as categorically bad for consumers
guest_hosts.credential_frame: A composite of independent analysts who track DRAM and NAND pricing cycles for institutional clients — the kind of person who gets quoted in trade press but doesn't work for any of the manufacturers
guest_hosts.delivery_baseline: Measured pace, slight edge of precision — like someone who has explained this three times this week and is choosing words carefully because the last person got it wrong
guest_hosts.expertise: Can explain why this shortage is structurally different from COVID-era disruptions, what HBM capacity allocation actually means for consumer DRAM supply, and why no fab currently under construction changes the consumer picture before 2028 at the earliest
guest_hosts.field: semiconductor supply chain and memory market analysis
guest_hosts.label: MAREN
guest_hosts.personality: Precise and a little impatient with soft framings — she'll correct 'shortage' to 'reallocation' without being asked, and she finds the gaming industry's surprise at this situation mildly exasperating. Not cold, but she doesn't perform warmth either. She'll give Juno credit when the instinct is right and tell Caspar when his skepticism is aimed at the wrong target.
guest_hosts.role_in_episode: Enters after the cold open establishes the price shock and before the hosts can settle into a 'this will probably sort itself out' rhythm. She closes the door on easy optimism, hands the hosts the structural argument, and exits before the SteamOS pivot — leaving Juno and Caspar to work out what it means without her

key_claims.claim: Valve raised Steam Deck OLED prices by $240-$300 on May 27, 2026, pushing the 512GB model from $549 to $789 and the 1TB from $649 to $949—a 43-46% increase on unchanged hardware

Valve programmer Pierre-Loup Griffais confirmed Steam Deck 2 is in development but stated they're 'not interested in 20, 30, or even 50% more performance'—they want a generational leap. Industry reports suggest a potential 2028 launch window.

AI data centers now consume 70% of high-end DRAM production. Samsung, SK Hynix, and Micron pivoted memory chip production to AI infrastructure, causing DDR5 32GB kits to triple from $100-200 to $350+. Memory shortages projected through at least Q4 2027, with some executives saying 2030.
The handheld gaming PC market fell from 2.87 million units shipped in 2023 to 1.49 million in 2024, before the worst of the shortage hit

Intel's Arc G3 Extreme processor (Panther Lake) features 14 CPU cores and 12 Xe3 GPU cores, with XeSS 3 multi-frame generation support. It's Intel's first chip specifically optimized for handheld gaming.

MSI Claw 8 EX AI+ launches June 23, 2026 with a reported $1,500 MSRP. Acer Predator Atlas 8 launches October 2026 with pricing expected around $1,200+. OneXPlayer 3 launches via Indiegogo mid-to-late June 2026.

Lenovo Legion Go 2 top-spec model (32GB RAM, 2TB) originally cost $1,479.99 but spiked to $2,849.99 amid the RAM crisis—a 92% price hike. The Legion Go S with SteamOS is now discontinued and temporarily unavailable.
SteamOS 3.9 brought official support for AMD-powered third-party handhelds including ASUS ROG Ally and Lenovo Legion Go, marking Valve's expansion beyond its own hardware
The Lenovo Legion Go S with SteamOS ($649) is currently considered the best value handheld gaming PC—it's the only third-party handheld to run SteamOS natively

AyaNeo suspended preorders for its Next 2 handheld ($2,299-$4,299) because storage prices increased 'several times higher' after Chinese New Year, making the product 'no longer sustainable' at current selling prices
